California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 21, 1990

Anne K. Mester
Public Utilities Counsel IV
State of California
Public Utilities Commission
505 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, CA 94102-3298

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-90-389
Follow-Up

Dear Ms. Mester:

This is to advise you that, upon consideration by the
Commission at its September 5, 1990 meeting, the advice we gave to
you in the Mester Advice Letter, No. A-90-389, dated July 17,
1990, has been revised.

In your letter to the Commission, you sought advice as to
whether an item received by each member of the California Public
Utilities Commission (the "CPUC") constituted a "gift", or, in the
alternative, fell under the "informational materials" exception to
the definition of gift under the Act. (Section 82028.) The item
involved was a scale model of a parabolic trough design solar
electric power plant collector plate. It had been given to the
CPUC Commissioners by a small power producer, Luz Development and
Finance Corporation ("Luz"), also known as Luz International
Limited. We informed you that the item was a gift.

The Commission considered the appeal that was filed by your
office on behalf of the CPUC Commission, and the oral and written
comments of interested persons who contended that the scale model
was incorrectly characterized as a gift. Based on the evidence in
the administrative record before it, the Commission determined
that the scale model in question should not be deemed a gift to
each of the CPUC commissioners but, rather, should be classified
as "informational materials" which are not gifts under the Act.
(Section 82029(b)(1).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Our File No. 90-389
Page 2


     Please contact me at (916) 322-5901 if you have any further
questions regarding this advice.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   [signature]
                                   By:  Deanne Stone
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:aa:90389f-up

LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY                                770 L STREET, SUITE 800                    SAN FRANCISCO
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000                  SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814               650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941               TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752                  SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                                                              TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800


                                        August 31, 1990


John H. Larson, Chairman
Members of the Commission
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95814

            Re:  Reconsideration of Mester Advice Letter
                 FPPC No. A-90-389

Dear Chairman Larson and Commissioners:

     This firm represents Luz International Limited ("Luz").
Attached to this letter you will find a memorandum regarding the
legal issues raised by the above-referenced matter. In this
letter, however, we would like to focus on the policy concerns
raised by the advice letter -- policy concerns which go well
beyond the scope of inquiry made by the Staff of the Commission.

     We are acutely aware of the legal issues raised by our
client's delivery of models of its solar collector to public
officials. We also understand and appreciate that the Commission
must adhere to precedent established through previous advice
letters and opinions, and must continue to develop its
interpretations of law in those important and complex areas.

     Nonetheless, we and our client are concerned that our
client's mistake and lack of sophistication in the Political
Reform Act area has caused a delay in state energy policy
implementation. And, as you know, this will not merely impact
our Luz' issues, but the interests of other energy producers, and
ultimately the public interest.

     Our experience with the Political Reform Act and its
history lends us to conclude that the intent of the voters in
enacting the Political Reform Act was not to hamstring government
in its essential operations. Our client's mistake has already
cost the taxpayers significantly because of the untold hours of
time spent by your staff and that of the Public Utilities
Commission ("CPUC"). Our client regrets this and only wishes it
knew then what it knows now--namely, that it should have sent
five of the models to the CPUC itself.

     We believe that at least five meritorious
interpretations of the law exist which would allow your

John H. Larson, Chairman
Members of the Commission
August 31, 1990
Page 2


Commission to do justice to its responsibilities to interpret the
statutes of the Political Reform Act in a sound but common sense
manner, and in a way calculated to insure a just result, while
also insuring minimum disruption of state business on crucial
energy issues. These options are discussed in greater detail in
the attachment. However, briefly stated, the options are as
follows:

     1.  The models provided to public officials can be
characterized as informational material provided to public
officials to understand production from renewable energy sources.

     2.  If the Commission were to characterize this model
as a gift, given the ambiguity on this issue, the Commission can
invoke the concept of constructive notice, and make the date of
the gift July 17, 1990, when the Commission staff informed the
CPUC of its conclusion.

     3.  The model can be characterized as both
informational (the model portion) and a gift (the name plate),
allowing for an apportionment of the gift amount.

     4.  Even if one were to conclude that the model can be
characterized only as a gift, the fair market value of the gift
is clearly much less than $250.00

     5.  If the Commission concludes that the model is a
January, 1990 gift valued above $250.00, the Commission can find
that the "true of necessity" applies because of the ambiguity
over whether this was indeed a gift at the time it was received.

     We have every confidence that you, as Commission
members, will be able to interpret the Political Reform Act in
a common sense and reasonable fashion which protects, rather than
impairs, the broad public interest at stake here.

     We thank you for your consideration.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        VIGO G. NIELSEN, JR.

VGN:kab

LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY                           270 L STREET, SUITE 800                    SAN FRANCISCO
                                       SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941                                                    650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800              TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752                   SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
                                                                                  TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800


                                 MEMORANDUM

                                August 30, 1990


TO:     John H. Larson, Chairman
        Fair Political Practices Commission
        Members of the Commission

FROM:   Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson, Parrinello & Mueller

RE:     Master Advice Letter Appeal

------------------------------------------------------------------------

        This memorandum is submitted on behalf of Luz
International Limited ("Luz").  A brief statement regarding the
background of the company and relevant additional facts, with
exhibits, is attached.

        The California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC") has
submitted an original and supplemental legal brief to you
regarding the issues raised.  Our focus is not to repeat but to
summarize.  Based on the law applicable to the particular facts
and circumstances present here, we request that the Commission
reconsider the advice rendered in Mester and direct the staff to
revise its advice accordingly.

        It is our position that there are a number of
meritorious interpretations of the law in this area which would
effectuate the relevant purposes of the Political Reform Act, but
which would not result in a virtual ban on the CPUC exercising
its necessary functions on matters relating to Luz.  We feel
most strongly that the models in question were not gifts, both
because of our client's intention in providing them, and because
of the information they provide about solar engineering.
However, there are additional arguments which we ask the
Commission to consider.

        1.  The model is not a gift, but informational material
provided to state public officials whose job, in part, is to
understand the mechanics of energy production from other than
disposable resources.  This would result in the models falling
within the "informational materials" exception to the definition
of gift in the Act (Gov't Code § 82028(b)), and would permit
CPUC commissioners to participate in decisions affecting Luz and
others who were not associated with Luz' delivery of the models.

The advice in Mester was rendered without your staff
having seen an actual model of Luz's LS-3 collector. Luz will
display one of these models at the September 5, 1990 meeting.
The models are bulky and unsuitable as a paperweight, desk
ornament or wall decoration. (c.f. In re Nida (1977) 3 FPPC
Ops. 1.) The models' only function is to demonstrate how Luz's
most current solar energy generation system works. This is the
use for which Luz designed them.

2. We acknowledge the possibility that the Commission,
for precedential reasons, may feel compelled to consider the
model a gift (as opposed to informational material). However,
given the appearance of the object and the facts and
circumstances present here, we think the Commission can
appreciate that recipients could be honestly confused about this
designation. Therefore, it would be logical and equitable to all
effected parties to make the date of receipt of the gift
July 17, 1990, when the FPPC informed the CPUC of its conclusion
regarding the character of the models. The 30 days allotted for
return of the models prescribed in the statute would run from
that date. This determination would permit the CPUC
commissioners to participate, as an item is not a gift, and thus
not a basis for disqualification or reportability, if it is returned
within 30 days of receipt. (Gov't Code § 82028(b).)

There is precedent for the Commission allowing
flexibility in "date of receipt" in a situation where there was a
misunderstanding about a gift. (Duffy Advice, A-84-84. The CPUC
has described this letter in its supplemental materials.) In
Duffy, the misunderstanding was about who gave the gift. Here,
there was confusion over whether there was a gift or whether the
model was informational. (The staff indicated that only value
was unclear and does not view the model as ambiguous in
character. (Staff memo at p. 9.)) However, staff also states
that the model does have "educational" components (p. 7).)

A directive to staff to apply the doctrine of
constructive receipt (and thus permit timely return) is a
solution that does not disturb existing precedents about what is
or is not a gift. Providing such flexibility regarding receipt
could well be based on all facts and circumstances present in
this particular case.

All members of the CPUC returned the solar collector
models to Luz within 30 days of being advised by FPPC staff in

_______________________

Luz understands that even if the models fall within the
"informational materials" exception (and thus are not gifts), the
models may possibly confer some personal benefit on the
recipients which will be reportable by Luz.

2

the Mester letter that the model was a gift and did not fall
within the informational materials exception.

     3. If any portion of the model is a gift, we believe
that it is only the personalized nameplate.

     The staff has stated in its memorandum that it "does
not dispute the fact that the model has an educational
component." (Staff memorandum at p. 7.) The staff then
discusses why, in its view, the "gift" component outweighs the
"informational materials" component, resulting in a gift. (Among
these reasons, the staff says the models were "personally given
to the CPUC commissioners during the winter holidays...." In
actuality, however, the models were sent via UPS on
January 2, 1990. They were sent then because the project using
the new solar technology demonstrated by the model had begun
generating power on December 28, 1989.)

     If an item has mixed characteristics, the Political
Reform Act does not require that one component (gift or
informational) predominate to the exclusion of the other. The
Consumers Union advice letter (Snyder Advice, No. A-85-131)
illustrates that an item can be both informational and of
personal benefit. The solution in that letter was to hold the
lobbyist-employer providing the free subscription to its magazine
accountable for the entire value of the subscription and the
recipient accountable for none of it. However, an apportionment
could be made so that the recipient would be accountable for the
part which has no informational attributes. Here, the
personalized nameplate (with a value of $7.50) is not
informational.

     4. If, despite the appearance of the model and its
inherent ambiguity of character, the Commission believes that
precedent requires you to consider the model exclusively as a
"gift," then the model could be valued at fair market value.
Here, a reasonable estimate of the market value of the model
clearly is less than $250.

     The Act requires that any object which is reportable be
valued at "fair market value." (Gov't Code § 82025.5.) The
staff has stated in its memorandum that the value of the LS-3
solar collector plate models is not at issue because Ms. Mester
of the CPUC informed the staff that the value was more than $250.
Ms. Mester's information was based on Luz's cost, which was more
than $250. However, the value to the recipient is the cost of
the item only if it is so unique or unusual that there is no
market equivalent. (Regulation 18726.) If similar items are
marketed, a public official may make a good faith estimate of the
fair market value. (In re Cory (1975)1 FPPC Ops. 153.)


                                3

Solar models are available on the market. Luz will
bring a commercially available model, and brochures regarding
such models, to the hearing. Luz paid more than market for its
models. Despite what Luz paid, the appearance of the models is
such that a good faith estimate of fair market value would not
approach the cost to Luz.

If the Commission finds that a good faith estimate of
the fair market value of the LS-3 solar collector models is less
than $250, then the CPUC could permissibly participate in
decisions affecting Luz. The estimated fair market value still
would be reportable by the recipients on their economic interest
statements (if the value is over $50, which it would be, based on
the commercially-available equivalents). Assuming the models
have some personal benefit, Luz would also report the models on
its lobbyist-employer report, but at cost to it.

5. We believe that even if the Commission were to find
the models to be a gift valued in excess of $250, there are sound
legal and policy reasons for the Commission concluding that the
rule of "legally required participation" applies where the
conditions of Government Code section 87101 are met. Application
of the rule would permit CPUC members, randomly selected, to
participate in the pending decisions affecting Luz (and others).
Both Luz and CPUC members would report the models, including the
commissioners chosen to participate.

The facts in the Hopkins Opinion can be distinguished
from the facts here. Hopkins only prohibited application of the
rule of legally required participation to those gifts that were
clearly gifts at the time of receipt and which were for the
enjoyment of the public official. Unlike the Disneyland tickets
or wine discussed in Hopkins, Luz's models have no entertainment
value and are not consumable.

Most important, in Hopkins, the donor (Disneyland) was
not involved in a complicated regulatory matter affecting
numerous other parties also likely to be affected. There, if the
Anaheim City Council could not vote on matters foreseeably and
materially affecting Disneyland, no one but Disneyland would
suffer if the rule of necessity permitting the city to continue
to conduct business did not apply. Here, that is not the case.
Although the staff states that "...the Act does not designate
equitable grounds, such as the doctrine of fairness, as a basis
for non-application of the Hopkins opinion", Luz believes the
Commission may consider issues of policy and equity without
express statutory authority.

Perhaps Luz may not be deserving of equity in light of
the fact that it caused this problem. Luz now realizes that the
potential conflict of interest and disqualification issues raised
by the Mester Advice letter could have been avoided had Luz sent

4

the models to the CPUC itself rather than to its members. (This
mechanism has apparently been employed to give items to the state
Assembly for display in legislators' offices. Milman Advise, I-
90-337.) However, at the time the models of its solar collector
plate were sent, Luz was not familiar with this method.

The penalty the Mester Advice letter imposes on Luz for
an inadvertent error is extremely severe, as a company of Luz's
size cannot easily bear months of construction delays. Penalties
will also attach to others not responsible for Luz's conduct. We
therefore request your reconsideration of the Mester Advice
letter.

JSD:kab
6558.01

Enclosures

5

BACKGROUND AND CHRONOLOGY
(With Exhibits)


Luz International Limited (collectively, "LIL"), a
solar energy producer, is the parent company of five Luz
subsidiaries. Each subsidiary has its own chief executive and
staff. All have a great deal of management autonomy. Within
each subsidiary, Luz employs an entrepreneurial management style
which encourages independent decision-making. This management
structure is necessary because Luz must annually design, obtain
permits for, develop, finance, construct and operate a large
(80 MW) utility-scale solar energy generating system (SEGS).

The program to distribute models of its state-of-the-
art solar collector originated in Luz's subsidiary, Luz Finance
and Development Corporation (LFD).

The models at issue in Mester were built to scale and
were constructed to move so that the solar technology could be
demonstrated. Each model sent to CPUC members and other
recipients was accompanied by a color diagram showing how the
parabolic solar collector was integrated into Luz's power
generating system. An original such diagram is attached as
Exhibit A.

The models demonstrated for the first time the third
generation (LS-3) of solar technology used by Luz. The models
were sent after the start-up on December 28th of the SEGS VIII
project, the eighth Luz solar project. SEGS VIII was the first
project where the LS-3 technology was used for the entire solar
field, and the first 80 MW solar project. The January 2nd
distribution (which was after the holidays) was intended to
coincide with this event.

On the average, the solar collector models cost Luz
Finance and Development Corporation $306 each, without the
nameplate and the Luz promotional plate and without shipping
charges. The value of the personalized nameplate was $7.50.

The total number of LS-3 models Luz Finance distributed
was 127. A list is attached as Exhibit B. The recipients
included people in other Luz subsidiaries, participants in Luz
projects, Luz's outside legal counsel and others with an interest
in solar technology. Relatively few were public officials.

The models were distributed with a form letter which
did not vary except for the addressee and salutation. A copy of
a sample letter is attached as Exhibit C.

The models for the CPUC commissioners were sent UPS,
second day delivery guaranteed, on January 2, 1990.

LUZ DEVELOPMENT AND FINANCE CORPORATION

924 Westwood Boulevard
Suite 1000
Los Angeles, CA 90024
213 205-7444
Telex 181515 LUZ LA
Facsimile 213 824-7843

Date

LUZ

PLEASE
UNWRAP
CAREFULLY!

Dear : [illegible]

We are pleased to announce that SEGS VIII, the largest solar electric
generating plant in the world, has been placed in service. With the
addition of SEGS VIII, LUZ's eight solar projects now provide enough clean,
renewable electricity to serve over 135,000 residential customers.

The cornerstone of LUZ's current technology is the new LS-3 solar collector.
Please accept this model of the LS-3 collector as a symbol of our sincere
appreciation of your support of LUZ, SEGS VIII, and the future of solar
technology.

Best regards,


James F. Verhey, President
Luz Development and Finance Corporation

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION
505 VAN NESS AVENUE
SAN FRANCISCO, CA  94102-3298


                            July 20, 1990


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     I enclose herewith the California Public Utilities Commission's
(CPUC's) appeal to the Fair Political Practices Commission's
(FPPC's) Advice Letter No. A-90-389, dated July 17, 1990. That
letter concluded that models of a parabolic trough design solar
electric power plant collector plate, which were provided to each
member of the CPUC, are gifts and do not fall under the
"informational materials" exception to the definition of gift
under the Act.  The Advice Letter also pointed out in footnote 7,
page 6, that because gifts were involved, the "rule of
necessity," codified at Government Code Section 87101, could not
be applied to the facts presented by the CPUC.  (In re Hopkins, 3
FPPC Ops. 107.)

     The CPUC's primary argument is that the models have been
incorrectly characterized as gifts.  Furthermore, even if the
FPPC continues to categorize the models as gifts, the FPPC should
let the CPUC cure the problem by letting the Commissioners return
the models immediately.  In addition, the FPPC should find that
the holding of the Hopkins opinion on the "rule of necessity"
should not be applied to this set of facts.

     The CPUC requests that its appeal be placed on the agenda of the
Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) meeting of August 7,
1990, and that the CPUC be given an opportunity to make a
presentation to the FPPC at that time.  As explained in the
attached appeal, because of the severe time constraints which
govern the proceeding currently before the CPUC which is
directly affected by the FPPC's Advice Letter, the CPUC urges the
FPPC to act on this appeal, if at all possible, on August 7.

     Sincerely,

     [signature]

     Anne K. Mester
     Public Utilities Counsel IV

BEFORE THE CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION




APPEAL OF THE CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION
TO THE CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION'S
ADVICE LETTER NO. A-90-389




JANICE E. KERR
MICHAEL B. DAY
ANNE K. MESTER

505 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, CA  94102
(415) 557-2380

Attorneys for the Public
Utilities Commission of
DATED:  July 20, 1990  the State of California

INTRODUCTION

The California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) hereby
appeals the Fair Political Practices Commission's (FPPC's) Advice
Letter No. A-90-389, issued July 17, 1990. That Advice Letter
concludes that scale models of a parabolic trough design solar
electric power plant collector plate, which were provided to each
member of the CPUC by the Luz Development and Finance Corporation
(Luz), are gifts and do not fall under the "informational
materials" exception to the definition of "gift" under the
Political Reform Act (Act). The Advice Letter also points out in
a footnote that because gifts are involved, the "rule of
necessity," codified at Government Code Section 87101, cannot be
applied to the facts presented by the CPUC. (In re Hopkins, 3
FPPC Ops. 107.)

For the reasons set forth below, the CPUC contends that the
models have been incorrectly characterized as gifts, and that
even if it is assumed that they are gifts, the Hopkins
holding on the "rule of necessity" should not be applied to this
set of facts.

The CPUC requests that the FPPC find that the models are not
gifts. If the FPPC continues to categorize the models as gifts,
the CPUC requests that the FPPC allow the CPUC to cure the
problem by letting the Commissioners return the models
immediately. In addition, the CPUC requests the FPPC to find
that the facts presented by the CPUC should not be governed by
the holding of Hopkins on the "rule of necessity." Because of
the severe time constraints which govern the proceeding currently
before the CPUC and affected by the FPPC's Advice Letter, the
CPUC urges that if at all possible, the FPPC be prepared to take
action on this appeal at its meeting of August 7, 1990.

STATUS OF PROCEEDING AFFECTED BY THE ADVICE LETTER

Application 89-03-026 was filed by the Southern California
Edison Company (Edison) on March 20, 1989 for permission to
construct a 220 kilovolt (kV) double circuit transmission line
from the Kramer Junction to Edison's Victor substation in San
Bernardino County. Edison's estimate of the cost of this project
is $50.38 million. This line is being built to integrate and
interconnect the generation of two independent power producers,
Luz and California Energy Company (Cal Energy), into Edison's
transmission network. Luz' and Cal Energy's generation will be
used by Edison to serve its existing customers and will enable
Edison to avoid using its own facilities to produce the
electricity it is purchasing from Luz and Cal Energy. Pursuant
to the Public Utilities Regulatory Policies Act (PURPA: P.L. 95-
617, 92 Stat. 3117) and the regulations issued by the Federal
Energy Regulatory Commission for implementation of PURPA (18 CFR
Section 292.303), Edison has an obligation to interconnect and to
purchase the generation of Luz and Cal Energy. The Kramer-Victor
line will enable Edison to carry out this statutory
responsibility.

2

The CPUC held 11 days of hearing in the above application
proceeding, during which 48 exhibits were filed and 95 pages of
testimony amassed, and was prepared to issue a decision at its
regular meeting on July 18, 1990. The CPUC received the FPPC
Staff's Advice Letter on July 17 and was thus unable to vote.

The Permit Streamlining Act, Government Code Sections 65920
et seq., provides that if the CPUC did not act by July 19, the
application would be deemed approved by operation of law.
Because of the compelling circumstances presented by the Advice
Letter, this date has been extended for ninety days.

ARGUMENT

I. The Solar Models Are Not Gifts.

The Advice Letter concludes that the models, which were
provided to each member of the CPUC by Luz, are gifts and do not
fall under the "informational materials" exception to the
definition of "gift" under the Act. The CPUC strongly disagrees
with this conclusion.

As stated in our letter of May 29, 1990, when the CPUC
Commissioners received the models, they all regarded them as
educational devices. They were model replicas of power plants
utilizing state of the art technology to produce solar thermal
electricity. They were accompanied by a sheet explaining how the
luz system, as exemplified by the model, operated. They offered
our Commissioners not only the opportunity to learn about the
technology -- which, because it is a major component of the
independent power production industry, is directly related to the
CPUC's regulatory responsibilities -- but the further opportunity
to be able to demonstrate it to others. They have, in fact, made
such educational use of the models on numerous occasions.

Furthermore, our Commissioners never considered that there
was any possibility that the models would be determined to be
prohibited gifts because, apart from their educational value,
they were believed to have only nominal personal financial value.
It came as a total surprise when the Commissioners learned, well
after 30 days from receipt, that the "cost" to Luz was reported
to be over $300 per model. However, as soon as they received
this information, they immediately requested their attorneys to
have the matter clarified with the FPPC.

Even though Luz has reported its cost to be over $300, the
value to each Commissioner is practically nothing. In Re Cory, 1
FPPC ops. 153, held that in terms of valuation of unique gifts,
it is not necessary to have an appraiser assess the value of the
item; all the FPPC will require is a good faith effort on the
part of agency officials to place a value on an item whose fair
market value cannot be easily ascertained. Even had there been
some ambiguity in the CPUC Commissioners' minds over the status
of the models, they in good faith believed them to have a value
of well below $250. The models cannot be resold for anything
more than a very minor amount; they are not of the nature of art

3

objects; they are not like season tickets or free passes (whether
or not those items are valued in terms of their maximum potential
use or in terms of each actual use); moreover, the quality of
materials and workmanship indicates a value well below $250.
Even had the Commissioners considered the models to be
commemorative plaques (as the nameplates might suggest), the
Commissioners had no good reason to believe their value exceeded
$250.

     This does not mean that the models do not have some
intrinsic value.  However, in the instance where the models have
virtually no personal value which would create some sense of
indebtedness in the recipient, but do, in fact, have a
substantial educational value which creates no sense of
indebtedness but serves the public interest -- it is manifestly
unfair to use the cost to the donor as the sole figure for
valuation purposes.  While the models' institutional value is
substantial, their personal value is minimal, and should
certainly be considered to be well below the $250 limit for
disqualification purposes.

     The CPUC remains of the view that the models fall into the
"informational materials" exception to the definition of "gift."
The notion of "gift," as defined in the Act, is that something is
given to someone without any reciprocal consideration flowing
back to the donor.  But this is true of informational materials
as well as any other tangible or intangible item.  The apparent
key to the distinction, as discussed by the FPPC on page 4 of the
Advice Letter, is that no personal benefit accrues to an
individual recipient from receipt of an informational material;
rather, the benefit flows to the agency itself (as, for example,
with the tours which the FPPC has found to be informational).

     Under this standard, the models clearly fit under the
informational category.  They are visual representations of
important solar technology, which, as part of the small power production industry in California, is directly related to the CPUC's regulatory responsibilities.  This
technology has been described in a variety of newspapers and
periodicals, which has served to heighten interest in its design
and use.  Thus our Commissioners are often asked questions about
the technology.  The models provide a substitute for a tour of
the actual solar facilities, and in addition to enabling our
Commissioners to understand the technology, have been used by
them to demonstrate it to others.

     As much benefit flows to the CPUC from each of our
Commissioners having one of these models as flows from individual
agency members touring a power plant site.  Moreover, the FPPC's
view that no benefit flows to the individual official from a
plant tour is incorrect; while the collective agency knowledge
bank is enriched, it is only through the knowledge gained by
individual officials that this benefit is realized.  This is also
true with the solar models.  However, in both cases, very little
personal financial benefit flows to the individual recipients;

4

what little benefit of that nature may exist is overshadowed by
the educational, institutional benefit.

The FPPC appears to regard the fact that the models had
personalized nameplates as highly important to its determination
that they are gifts. Surely had the official who had a power
plant tour set up especially for him been given a name tag, the
FPPC would not have decided the tour was a gift. Moreover, the
nameplates do nothing to detract from the educational value of
the models; in fact, it can be argued that the only way they can
achieve their maximum educational potential is to be given to
those individuals who need most to understand the technology they
are making decisions on -- the Commissioners. Moreover, in a
large and busy office like the CPUC, nameplates may be the only
means of ensuring that the models are available when needed by a
Commissioner.

In light of the above facts, it is manifestly unfair to
rigidly apply the rule that the models must have been returned to
LU2 within 30 days of receipt in order for the CPUC Commissioners
to avoid disqualification. Such an application could well place
a chilling effect on information-gathering in the public sphere.

II. The Holding In The Hopkins Opinion On The Rule Of Necessity
Should Not Be Applied To The Facts Of This Case.

In re Hopkins, 3 FPPC Ops. 107, on a 3-2 vote, holds in
pertinent part that the statutory rule of necessity, Govt. Code
Section 87101,[1] may not be invoked to allow a disqualified
official to vote on a decision if the basis of that
disqualification is a gift from a donor financially interested in
that decision, as opposed to income from or investment in that
same entity, and if it could be reasonably foreseen at the time
the gift was received that the official would be asked to make or
participate in making a governmental decision affecting the
donor.

Section 87101 provides:

"Section 87100 [the basic disqualification section] does
not prevent any public official from making or
participating in the making of a governmental decision to_
the extent his participation is legally required for the
action or decision to be made. The fact that an
official's vote is needed to break a tie does not make his
participation legally required for purposes of this
section." (Emphasis added.)

The FPPC has enacted a regulation implementing Section 87101
which states, in pertinent part:

1 Unless otherwise specified, statutory references are to the
Government Code.

5

"A public official is not legally required to make or to
participate in the making of a governmental decision
within the meaning of Government Code Section 87101 unless
there exists no alternative source of decision consistent
with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the
decision." (Emphasis added.)

2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18701(a).

The CPUC contends that application of the rule set forth in
Hopkins to the instant facts will lead to the "mischief"
predicted by FPPC two dissenters to that opinion, and that
even if the FPPC continues to categorize the solar models as
gifts, the Hopkins holding on the rule should not be applied to
the facts of this case.

In Hopkins, the items involved were never seriously
considered to be anything but gifts; the primary question was how
they should be valued. Here, the CPUC never seriously considered
the models to be gifts; from the date of their receipt, our
Commissioners, in good faith, viewed them as working models of a
technology which was relevant to the CPUC's regulatory
responsibilities, of an educational/informational nature, and of
a value considerably less than $250. While it may have been
reasonably foreseeable at the time of receipt of the models that
a decision affecting Luz could be before the CPUC within the
year, it was never reasonably foreseeable that the models would
be viewed as prohibited gifts.

Secondly, the Hopkins opinion was applied prospectively; the
FPPC recognized that the councilmembers requesting the advice in
that case might well have received tickets and passes which,
under the standard of valuation used in that opinion, would
subject them to disqualification. Since they could not return
them within the 30 days required by the Act, applying the opinion
retrospectively would be unfair and would "unduly hamper the
operations of the city council." Hopkins, 3 FPPC Ops. at 117.
Thus the opinion was only applied prospectively, and not to any
passes or tickets already received which were returned promptly
to their donors. Fairness requires the same result here. The
CPUC Commissioners, in good faith, believed the models to be
educational, and not gifts. They also, in good faith, believed
them to be of nominal personal value. However, out of an
abundance of caution, as soon as they became aware of Luz'
valuation figure, they sought advice from the FPPC. In the face
of these facts, to now apply a finding that the models are gifts
which subject the CPUC Commissioners to disqualification, with no
opportunity to return them, would be manifestly unfair.

Finally, the Hopkins opinion occurred in the context of the
ordinary business of a city council. There were no unusual
circumstances presented which required the FPPC to consider how
the concept of "legally required participation" might be
construed under those circumstances. In the instant case,
unusual circumstances exist. The decision before the CPUC is not
just ordinary day-to-day business. This decision is governed by

6

sections.question: s regarding this advice.
sections.conclusion:
For all of the above reasons, the cPuc urges the FPPC to
find that the solar models are not gifts. 
If the FPPC declines
to make this finding, the CPUC requests it to allow the
Cornmissj.oners to cure the problem by returning the nodels
innediately. In addition, the CPUC requests the FPPC to find
that the holding in Hopkins on the 'rule of necessity' is not
appLicable to the facts of this case.
Respectfully subnritted,
JANICE E. KEP'R
MTCHAEL B. DAY
ANNE K. MESTER
July 20, 199 0

erhaps Luz may not be deserving of equity in f.ight of

Dear IiIr. HalLabrin:
I enclose herewith the california Public Utilities 
commission's
(cPUc's) appeal to the Fair Political Practices Cornmission's
(FPPc's) Advice Letter No. A-90-389, dated July L7, 1990. That
letter concluded that models of a parabolic trough design solar
electric polrer plant collector plate, which were provided to each
nember of the CPUC, are gifts and do not faII under the
'informati,onal materials" exception to the definition of gift
under the Act. The Advice Letter also pointed out in footnote 7,, that because gifts were involved, the 'rule of
necessity,' codified at Government code section 87101., could not
be applied to the facts presented by the CPUC. (In re Hopkins, 3
FPPC ops. 1,07. )
The cPuc's primary arg"ument is that the nodels have been
incorrectly characterized as gifts. 
rurthermore, even if the
FPPC continues to categorize the models as gifts, the FPPC should
1et the cPUc cure the problen by letting the Comrnissioners return
the models irurediately. In additj.on, the FPPC should find that
the holding of the Hopkins opinion on the 'rul-e of necessity"
should not be applied to this set of facts.
The cPUc requests that its appeal be placed on the agenda of the
Fair Political Practices Cornrnission (FPPC) meeting of August 7,
1990, and that the cPUc be given an opportunity to make a
presentation to the FPPC at that tine. 
As explained in the
attached appeal , because of the severe time constraints
which govern the proceeding currently before the CPUC which is
directly affected by the FPPC'S Advice Letter, the CPUC urges the
FPPC to act on this appeal, if at all possible, on August 7.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission November 21, 1990 Anne K. Mester Public Utilities Counsel IV State of California Public Utilities Commission 505 Van Ness Avenue San Francisco, CA 94102-3298 Re: Your Request For Advice Our File No. A-90-389 Follow-Up Dear Ms. Mester: This is to advise you that, upon consideration by the Commission at its September 5, 1990 meeting, the advice we gave to you in the Mester Advice Letter, No. A-90-389, dated July 17, 1990, has been revised. In your letter to the Commission, you sought advice as to whether an item received by each member of the California Public Utilities Commission (the "CPUC") constituted a "gift", or, in the alternative, fell under the "informational materials" exception to the definition of gift under the Act. (Section 82028.) The item involved was a scale model of a parabolic trough design solar electric power plant collector plate. It had been given to the CPUC Commissioners by a small power producer, Luz Development and Finance Corporation ("Luz"), also known as Luz International Limited. We informed you that the item was a gift. The Commission considered the appeal that was filed by your office on behalf of the CPUC Commission, and the oral and written comments of interested persons who contended that the scale model was incorrectly characterized as a gift. Based on the evidence in the administrative record before it, the Commission determined that the scale model in question should not be deemed a gift to each of the CPUC commissioners but, rather, should be classified as "informational materials" which are not gifts under the Act.
